topic: Fast Cash Plays via Underutilized Assets

text: Define problem terms before prescribing solutions: ask What does it mean?, How do you know?, and Why do I care? for every problem statement before moving to tactics.
Test a fast cash play first with a handful of beta users before building it into standard onboarding, to verify the campaign design before scaling.
topic: Fast Cash Plays via Underutilized Assets

text: For high-volume organic inbound funnels, insert a video sales letter or written indoctrination step before the booking page to filter prospects and pre-frame the offer before the sales call.
topic: VSL as Qualification and Pre-Frame Gate

text: Require every sales rep to deliver the approved script verbatim on every call; any rep who improvises or goes off-script is removed from the call queue.
Run daily sales training sessions to verify reps are saying phrases the right way and have the script fully memorized.
Manage sales reps on a daily and weekly basis with explicit career-goal conversations, morning routines, and scripted management cadences.
Send the script to sales applicants before any interview and require a 60-second audition video as the first filter step.
Use group drill calls instead of one-on-one interviews to screen multiple candidates simultaneously for coachability and script readiness.
If individual tactics and script enforcement fail to produce adherence, diagnose the sales manager as the root constraint and consider replacing them or rebuilding the team from scratch.
topic: Leadership Sets Culture; Culture Determines Acceptable Effort

text: Before attempting to scale marketing spend, install tracking: know the channel breakdown of every paid dollar, the number of conversations required per sale, and the cost per customer acquired.

For service businesses that receive most work through intermediaries (agencies, brokers, producers), build a structured partner onboarding and activation journey — define what an activated partner looks like and measure partner churn separately from end-customer churn.

When designing a growth strategy for episodic-work businesses, target acquiring one new distribution partner per month rather than optimizing for end-customer volume; track partner count and partner activation rate as primary KPIs.

Show agencies and producers how much incremental revenue or margin they capture when they bundle your service into their client proposals; this is the primary motivator for a distributor to refer actively rather than episodically.
Collect bank statements or earnings documentation from every graduate six months after they complete the program, using a rebate or access benefit as the incentive to submit.

When pitching universities for partnerships, always ask: 'What would it take to make this exclusive? What would it take to lock in for five years?' to convert vendor conversations into monopoly-tier distribution agreements.

Attend trade shows and academic conferences in the target institutional market (e.g., BCBA professional conferences) as the primary cold outreach channel to find and build relationships with university program directors.
topic: Conferences and Events as High-Leverage Channels

text: To accelerate university partnership pipeline, hire a salesperson who has previously sold into academia and already has relationships ('black book') with key decision-holders at target universities.

Build an alumni job board as the high-value post-completion benefit; gate lifetime access on submitting post-graduation earnings data to generate the outcomes documentation needed for institutional sales.

For a certification test-prep business, present to universities using documented graduate earnings statistics as the primary pitch asset, since universities want to advertise those numbers to their own prospective students.

To build a brand fast: start with a brand that means nothing, identify what your ideal customers already like (people, experiences, values, outcomes), deliberately pair your brand with those things, and allow the association to transfer. Repeat until the brand strongly means the desired thing to the target audience.

When evaluating whether a brand pairing decision was good or bad, measure it objectively by whether it netted more or fewer sales among your ideal customer segment. Do not use subjective or personal opinion as the arbiter.

Choose brand associations (people, events, values) that your specific ideal customer segment likes, not associations that are generically popular. A pairing good for one audience may be bad branding for your audience.
Identify the consumable portion of your community's value separately from the one-time method or knowledge transfer, then price recurring membership against the consumable component only.
If you sell courses or webinars as one-time products, price them relative to the one-time value delivered; do not expect one-time content to sustain ongoing membership retention.

If your business has none of the five advantages, start with retention first and backfill the other four. If your industry structurally prevents retention, switching to an industry that does allow retention may be worthwhile early in your career.

Focus all retention effort on making the first 30 days excellent; that is the highest-leverage window for reducing membership churn. After month one, focus on getting members to month three, then walk them to month six.

Do not immediately switch businesses upon watching a framework like this; instead, identify the levers available within your current business to improve your standing on each dimension and apply them.

When qualifying customers for a recurring service, ensure the person you sell to actually has an ongoing need for the service; selling to customers who do not need recurring delivery is a structural source of voluntary churn.

If you want to raise capital or recruit franchisees and are struggling, audit the core unit economics first before improving marketing or sales strategy; bad economics, not bad pitch, is the most common root cause.

When growing a business that has genuine network effects, prioritize reinvesting cash into capturing market share over taking distributions, because the ROIC on reinvested capital in a network-effect business exceeds alternative uses.

When documenting your founding story publicly, use visceral sensory details — sounds, temperatures, exact dollar amounts, specific humiliations — rather than abstract hardship claims; specific details that would be embarrassing to fabricate are the highest-credibility proof format.

When framing your early-stage inaccessibility to a prospect as a trust signal, lead with your physical commitment to the space rather than your credentials — demonstrating that you cannot leave is more credible than explaining why you will stay.

Start workflow mapping at the highest level (5-6 macro stages) and then decompose each macro stage into its constituent sub-actions until no further reduction is possible; only then identify which sub-actions to automate.

Hire an AI automation specialist (generalist who connects tools) as a high-leverage investment once cash flow allows; deploy them to sit alongside teams and surface automatable tasks across every business function.

When specifying tasks for AI agents, use only observable, black-and-white language (e.g., 'raise your voice,' 'talk faster,' 'nod when the other person talks') and eliminate all vague abstractions (e.g., 'be more charismatic,' 'maintain brand quality') from AI prompts.
Prioritize automating back-office administrative tasks (invoicing, receivables, lead nurture) for in-person and home-services businesses first, as current AI tooling already handles these reliably.
Update AI agent prompts on a continuous basis exactly as you would update training instructions for a human employee when you discover a gap or improvement in their behavior.

When facing a constraint (hiring speed, revenue growth, pricing), ask what physical law of reality prevents achieving the goal; if no physical law prevents it, treat other objections as mental handicaps that competitors are welcome to keep.

When a goal appears blocked, generate at least a dozen distinct attack vectors before concluding it is impossible: network bounty offers, acquisitions of SDR teams, hiring additional recruiters, flying candidates in for accelerated in-person onboarding, leveraging senior reps with bandwidth, and so on.
Do not apologize for unreasonable goals; stubbornness and refusal to seek consensus are necessary traits for capturing outsized returns, not character flaws to be corrected.
To achieve what others cannot, reject the vast majority of what others do by definition; measure performance against physics and math, not industry benchmarks.
When breaking down any problem, apply the three-question sequence in order: (1) What does this mean? (2) How do we know? (3) Why does it matter? — corresponding to logic, evidence, and utility.
Before accepting or using any abstract term in a high-stakes conversation or analysis, define it operationally: state what observable conditions would prove it true or false.

Before hiring a GM to run a service-business location, role-play operational crises with the candidate and require them to handle each scenario without calling you; only candidates who can do this without hesitation should be hired.

When handing over a facility to a GM, put the GM's picture on the wall and refer to it as their facility, not yours, to create psychological ownership that drives above-and-beyond operational behavior.

For rollup deal structures, consult an advisor on restructuring options before pitching operators, as a few structural changes can simultaneously improve your tax position and make the deal appear more attractive to target operators.
When doing cold outreach, post on the same platform you are using for outreach (e.g., if outreaching on LinkedIn, post LinkedIn content) so that your profile is current when prospects look you up.
Never send pricing to a prospect via text or email after viewing their property or situation; show up in person and present pricing as a strategy you will deploy for them.
topic: Warm vs. Cold Sales Process Mismatch

text: Treat every outreach prospect as though they have never heard of you: always include your name, your company, your track record, and the specific mechanism you used to get results before making your ask.
In early stage, run cold outreach and baseline content creation in parallel: outreach for consistent pipeline, content for credibility nurture and occasional inbound deals.

When evaluating a founder-led business as an investor, ask immediately whether the founder has a housekeeper and has delegated domestic tasks; if not, the first capital recommendation is to fund that delegation.

For earners above $70k/year, delegate all domestic time-cost activities (driving, food preparation, home cleaning, clothing care) before upgrading status goods like house or car, since time recovery produces larger quality-of-life returns per dollar.

If a service objection is blocking a high-value client segment, first attempt to sell through the objection by reframing it; only fall back to audience restriction or delivery redesign if the reframe fails.

When a LinkedIn DM agency cannot gain personal-account access from a founder, explore whether DMs from the company brand account achieve sufficient volume to make the service viable at a lower per-send conversion rate.

Ask a hesitant founder-CEO: what is your objective as a CEO, and would your brand be stronger if your business were bigger? Use their answer to reframe outbound DM services as brand-building, not brand-risking.
Translate every standard you hold for your team into a specific, observable action — not a value or feeling, but a behavior you can see and measure.
Before requiring any behavior of your team, audit whether you personally model that behavior; if you do not, make the change in yourself first.

Build a universal membership enrollment motion so that every patient leaving a med spa visit is presented with a membership option; aim for a very high percentage of patients converting to membership to lock in scheduling predictability and recurring revenue.
Structure injector or provider career paths explicitly (levels, compensation milestones, leadership tracks) to increase loyalty to the brand rather than to the individual practitioner.
Pilot paid acquisition (PPC, paid social) before relying on organic-only growth in high-demand markets; organic demand is market-dependent and not a replicable system.
Pivot away from large-footprint wellness center concepts toward smaller, higher-ROI, tightly scoped locations (1,200–1,500 sq ft) focused on the highest-margin services, then replicate the model.

Before pursuing top-of-funnel content or marketing expansion, fix the sales process first: if close rate is around 10% on warm referral leads, fixing the process likely triples revenue before any additional acquisition spend.

For a center-of-influence-based business, rebuild all acquisition metrics around cost-per-acquired-affiliate (center of influence), not cost-per-acquired-end-client; orient all marketing messaging to attract and onboard referral partners.

When launching a new affiliate partner, offer to do initial work for free in exchange for the right to use the affiliate's experience as a case study; alternatively, stipulate that the launch requires the affiliate to refer at least 10 clients so an internal proof set can be generated.

To integrate an affiliate fully, identify where in their client onboarding workflow the referral can be embedded, so that every new client of the affiliate automatically enters your pipeline rather than only those the affiliate chooses to refer selectively.

Give each affiliate partner a repeatable pitch formula they can include in their own sales process (e.g., a CPA can say: my clients also save X% on health benefits through our partner), because embedding the offer into the affiliate's pitch is more sustainable than relying on the affiliate to independently remember to refer clients.

When pitching a value proposition that sounds too good to be true, anchor credibility by naming the industry average profit margin and showing structurally how your model eliminates it; this converts an unbelievable outcome claim into a believable structural explanation.

Before consuming any piece of content — podcast, book, video — ask: what specific behavior am I going to change as a result of this? If the answer is nothing, skip it or treat it explicitly as entertainment, not learning.

When trying to predict or influence another person's behavior, diagnose what they are deprived of relative to their reference group rather than asking how to motivate them. Deprivation identification yields stronger behavioral predictions than motivational framing.
Spend money wherever you can to learn more, and set up your life so that by default you are continuously increasing your knowledge exposure.

When in a business partnership, read the same books, attend the same events, and share the cliff notes whenever one partner consumes content the other has not yet seen — so neither partner operates from an information asymmetry.

Stop selling from your own wallet. If you come from a low-wealth background, your intuitions about what prices are reasonable are calibrated to the wrong buyers. Assume the person across from you has money.

When creating an upsell tier, ensure it is 5–10x the price of the core offer and that you would be genuinely satisfied delivering it at that price. If delivery feels like too much work, either do less or charge more.
topic: Pareto Pricing: Four-Tier Structure at 5–10x Gaps

text: Expect only 1 in 5 to 1 in 10 prospects to say yes to a premium upsell. Optimizing for the most yeses is not the same as optimizing for the most money; premium offers require accepting a high no rate.
topic: Pareto Pricing: Four-Tier Structure at 5–10x Gaps

text: If you are in person and literally cannot say a high price without choking, write it down and slide it to them, or show it on a calculator. Use physical distance between you and the number to reduce vocal hesitation.

Before stating a high price, say the words it’s super expensive. This pre-frames the anchor for wealthy buyers (who imagine a higher number) and pre-braces lower-budget buyers, while giving the salesperson a breath to compose themselves.

Check your close rate before assuming your price is correct or that sales skill is the problem. Apply Alex Hormozi’s rubric: above 60% signals underpricing, 30–40% signals appropriate pricing, below 30% signals a qualification or sales skill gap.
topic: Close Rate as Pricing Calibration Signal

text: Start building a product line or advisory practice as high up the pricing ladder as you can operationally support, then add lower tiers over time. Do not start cheap and try to move premium; the brand narrative is much harder to reverse.

To qualify buyers for premium offers, signal in all marketing that you only serve a specific type of high-budget customer. Let avatar clarity self-select the correct prospects before any sales conversation begins.
Before pivoting to a new customer segment, spend at least one month running your acquisition system in that segment to collect enough LTV data to calculate a real LTV-to-CAC ratio.
When choosing between two different-avatar businesses, do not attempt to run both indefinitely; identify which has the better LTV-to-CAC ratio and sunset the weaker one.
When you are willing to give up the current smaller business (or the portion serving a non-core avatar), treat that willingness as the entry condition for pursuing the higher-potential segment.
Avoid judging your own progress by the thoughts you have; track only the observable actions you take.
When feeling burnt out, treat it as an athletic training signal — verify no injury is present, then increase rather than decrease volume to build adaptation.
Train your sales team daily; a lack of daily training cadence is the primary execution gap between businesses with high close rates and those that cannot replicate founder performance.
topic: Daily Sales Role-Play as Training Cadence

text: Audit your existing sales script and cut half or more of its length; scripts are typically too long and include product information the rep cannot credibly deliver, which is not required for the prospect to make a purchasing decision.
Stop paying a rep commission on any sale that required your personal involvement to close; use commission withholding as the mechanism to break the habit of rep escalation to the founder.

When launching a new business or product line, begin with the highest-price, lowest-volume tier you can credibly deliver; only expand to higher-volume, lower-price tiers after establishing brand equity and operational capacity.
Avoid entering a market at the budget or mass-market tier if you intend to offer a premium product in the future; the low-price anchor is very difficult to overcome once set.
If you are a service business without automation, move pricing in the opposite direction of mass market: target the best customer at the highest possible price rather than competing for volume.
When adding a new pricing tier above your current core offer, make the jump at least 5x, not 50%, because buyers evaluate tiers categorically, not incrementally.
Accept that only a small percentage of prospects will take a very high-price tier, and be comfortable with that because even a small number at a high price is still significant revenue.

When a relationship partner or colleague asks you to change a behavior, first ask whether changing that behavior costs you anything significant and whether keeping it benefits you more than the relationship benefit of changing it; if the cost is negligible and the benefit to them is real, change the behavior without treating it as an identity threat.
Extend your attribution window from 30-60 days to 6-12 months when evaluating brand-building ad spend to see its true return on capital.

Shift thinking from quantitative performance-marketer mode (optimizing CTR, CPA, ROAS) to customer psychology mode (what associations does my target avatar find positive?) to execute brand building effectively.
Identify the flagship brand or product line with the highest brand potential and concentrate investment there before attempting to scale multiple undifferentiated product lines.

Build founder story, narrative differentiation, and top-of-funnel awareness content as the primary paid-media investment once the brand reaches a scale where direct-response-only spend begins compressing margins.

To attract the right customers through content, stack all four SPCL components (Status, Power, Credibility, Likeness) in your content strategy rather than optimizing for maximum view count or entertainment value.

When building an influence-based content strategy, define your terms operationally (by what is observable with your eyes) rather than using emotionally loaded language, to make your advice more actionable and your audience's results more predictable.

When creating content to build Power, make specific verifiable predictions or recommendations, allow your audience to act on them, and document the positive outcomes so each successful cycle compounds your future compliance rate.

Build a grand opening playbook for each new restaurant location that specifies overstaffing levels, multi-channel ad spend, and promotional giveaway structure (mass promotions for casual concepts, exclusive invite-only for premium concepts).
Before committing to a grand opening, validate that the food quality and overall experience are strong enough that word-of-mouth alone can sustain the location to full capacity within six months.

Reframe the restaurant marketing budget question for finance partners: instead of asking how much to spend on ads, ask how much premium is being paid across systems, service, and ingredients to increase word-of-mouth virality.
Send candidates the sales script before any interview and require a 60-second video of them reciting it as the first screening gate.
Run group coaching calls (not individual interviews) for sales rep candidates; drill the script live, give feedback, and observe whether candidates are coachable.
Require script memorization and verbatim delivery as a non-negotiable condition of employment for all reps; treat deviation as a fireable offense.
Conduct daily sales training sessions to keep all reps delivering the script correctly and consistently.
topic: Daily Sales Role-Play as Training Cadence

text: Before attempting a cultural gut-and-rebuild, implement the full sales ops checklist (script enforcement, daily training, daily huddles, career goal conversations); only escalate to mass termination if the checklist fails due to entrenched cultural resistance.

Hold the sales manager accountable as the primary lever for team performance — if the team has persistent culture problems, diagnose whether the manager is reinforcing the right behaviors before making rep-level changes.
topic: Leadership Sets Culture; Culture Determines Acceptable Effort

text: Whatever reason you need to give yourself to increase the likelihood that you take the required actions is sufficient justification for that reason, regardless of how the reason sounds or whether it is socially acceptable (e.g., jealousy, rage).

Define your input-output equation before increasing volume: identify the specific action that moves you toward your goal, then multiply that action by 10x. If you can reach 10x, figure out how to reach 100x.

Recognize that the control behaviors that made you successful as a self-employed person must be actively unlearned as you scale; treat relinquishing each business function (delivery, admin, sales, marketing, management, leadership, financing) as a sequential milestone rather than a loss.
When something bad happens, recover your emotional and behavioral baseline before talking to anyone else in your life — contain the setback before it spreads.
Use whatever motivational fuel you currently have — shame, deprivation, anger, aspiration — rather than waiting for a narratively 'acceptable' source of motivation. The rule is: use what you have.
Treat a bad day the same as any other day in terms of how you behave toward your spouse, children, and colleagues. A bad day is not moral permission to misbehave.

When one bad thing happens, actively prevent it from touching other life domains (health, marriage, parenting, business) by isolating it and not using it as justification for abandoning self-control in those other areas.
When scaling paid ads, sequence your moves: first make more creative, then increase budget, then expand to new platforms. Do not increase budget before building creative volume.

Before a major launch, bank a large creative library (e.g., 2,000+ ads) by hiring additional editors well in advance; do not rely on a small editorial team to generate launch-scale creative output in real time.

When trying to diagnose erratic or volatile sales, calculate the implied advertising volume needed to achieve your target sales frequency and compare it to your current volume — close the gap rather than investigating offer quality or market conditions first.

Commit to a fixed daily output target (calls, ads, content pieces) and hold it for an extended period even when short-term results are poor; the consistency itself builds the data and optimization pressure that makes the volume worthwhile.
When assessing the quality of your work, ask whether there is anything you can still remove, add, or clarify; only declare it done when the answer to all three is no.
Track your effort investment in a domain by counting in hundreds of hours rather than hours, to force engagement with the actual depth of mastery required.
When your work is submitted, ask: if I had two more weeks, would I approach this differently? If the answer is yes, you have not yet reached your attainable quality ceiling.
When you do not feel good about continuing, keep going anyway; the emotional state is temporary and will pass, while the work you complete in the meantime remains.
When assessing the value of a period of apparent failure, audit what skills and experiences you developed, not only what financial or external outcomes you produced.
Before attributing your uncompleted goals to circumstances (children, location, resources), trace the logic to its conclusion and identify who or what you are actually holding responsible.

When supporting someone through peak physical or psychological difficulty, use factual confirmation (you're fine) rather than motivational language (you got this) to provide more credible in-the-moment reassurance.

Before entering a high-stakes challenging situation, audit what you have already survived to set your personal worst-case baseline, then use it as the measuring stick against which all new threats are assessed.
When you need to learn a new skill faster than peers, identify how many iterations you require and schedule them as densely as possible (e.g., all in one day) rather than spacing them out over weeks.

If you feel outpaced by smarter peers, reframe the gap as an iteration-velocity problem, not a fixed-capacity problem, and respond by increasing daily practice volume rather than accepting the deficit as permanent.
topic: Effort Compounds Relative to Starting Conditions

text: Before adding new items to a membership offer, identify whether each item will be actively consumed on a monthly basis. If it will not be consumed, do not add it — unused items dilute perceived value rather than increasing it.

Map the work that members must currently do themselves to get value from your system, then take that work in-house: research, analyze, distill, and deliver the output as a monthly consumable. The deliverable should be immediately actionable with minimal additional effort from the member.

When designing a consumable media offering, identify both the cost the member saves by not having to test or research themselves, and the direct revenue the pre-vetted output generates. Use both figures to anchor the monthly price.

For behavior-change memberships (fitness, sales training, business coaching), diagnose whether your customers' primary bottleneck is knowledge or execution. If it is execution, build and pitch an accountability system — structured as peer-to-peer, alumni, and expert tiers — rather than adding more educational content.

Design consumable deliverables to feel magical: absorb all complexity internally so that the member receives only a ready-to-use output. The member should not see or feel the research, testing, and distillation work behind the deliverable.
